page.title: Welcome
page.subtitle: Overview of the PMCC Metastatic Planning Challenge series and how to get started
page.audience: Radiation therapists, dosimetrists and medical physicists

sections.title: Clinical Rationale
body_markdown_lines: Palliative VMAT planning presents a unique set of challenges. Complex target geometries, competing OAR priorities, limited published dose-constraint data, and fast turnaround expectations all converge — often in cases where the planner has had limited previous exposure to the specific clinical scenario.

At the same time, significant improvements in systemic therapies mean that many patients now live considerably longer than historical palliative cohorts. This has real consequences for planning: patients are more likely to experience late side effects from their radiation, and a growing number will become candidates for reirradiation. Plans that were once considered adequate for a short prognosis now need to account for a longer horizon.
Planning challenges let us practise this decision-making deliberately — with a safety net, a shared scoring language, and the space to develop judgement before it is needed under clinical pressure.

sections.title: Learning Objectives
body_markdown_lines: By the end of this challenge you will be able to:
1. **Respect hard OAR limits when the margin for error is thin** — the cord is near the BED₃ myelopathy threshold at this fractionation, so there is no room to negotiate on max dose.

2. **Make deliberate coverage trade-offs when multiple targets compete for the same OAR space** — three separate target volumes overlap the mediastinum, and every concession to one structure costs dose somewhere else.

3. **Control low-dose lung wash in a patient who cannot afford pneumonitis** — with an FEV₁ of 38% and no systemic therapy options, keeping Dmean and V16Gy low is not aspirational, it is the whole point of the plan.

sections.title: Challenge #1 — Thorax
body_markdown_lines: Clinicians frequently face a binary framing: conventional palliation (e.g. 30 Gy/10 fx, BED₁₀ = 39.0 Gy) versus radical-intent treatment (60 Gy/30 fx or SBRT). The 40 Gy in 10 fraction regimen (BED₁₀ = 56.0 Gy) addresses the critical **grey zone** — patients with large (>5 cm) or ultracentral thoracic tumours who need durable local control but cannot safely receive definitive chemoradiation or meet strict SBRT eligibility criteria.

The ~43% increase in biological dose over 30 Gy/10 fx pushes tumour control probability toward the ablative range for many NSCLC histologies, while retaining enough fractionation to treat large volumes safely. Published data (Chow et al. 2025; n = 62) demonstrate a 69.3% overall response rate, 96.7% one-year local control, and 76.9% three-year local control with an acceptable toxicity profile.
### What the safety data tell us

In the published cohort, 46.7% of patients developed Grade 1–2 radiation pneumonitis — a rate that reflects the large treatment volumes rather than clinical severity, as no Grade ≥3 pneumonitis occurred. The low incidence of significant oesophageal toxicity (1.6% Grade 3) confirms that modern VMAT technique can carve high-dose gradients safely in the mediastinum, provided OAR constraints are respected.

Despite the palliative intent, improvements in systemic therapy mean many patients now survive long enough to experience late normal-tissue effects or require retreatment to the same region. This has direct planning implications:
- **Minimise dose to serial OARs beyond what the scorecard requires** — every Grey saved now is retreatment headroom later.
- **Keep lung Dmean and V16Gy as low as achievable** — cumulative pneumonitis risk compounds with subsequent systemic immunotherapy cycles.
- **Document your plan clearly** — so a future planner or clinician can understand the dose distribution without re-planning from scratch.
This challenge asks you to plan as though the patient will live longer than historical palliative cohorts — because, increasingly, they do.
